dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for an unknown problem-person down at Walmart.
Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What happened just before the call came in?
He was not responsive. He had a seizure and fell down twice before we called 911.
He appears unresponsive. Could you confirm the time the first event happened?
We saw him go down first around 15 minutes ago, and a second time shortly after that.
Have you noticed any head or eye movement since then?
I think he moved to the right side, maybe an arm or leg, when the seizure happened earlier.
His airway is patent and he is able to maintain it on his own.
Breathing is unlabored, regular, and present on inspection and auscultation.
Radial pulses are palpable, normal in rate and strength with a regular rhythm observed.
He is currently A&O x0 based on initial field assessment.
His pulse is 98 beats per minute with regular beat.
His respiratory rate is 23 breaths per minute and unlabored.
Blood pressure is 148 over 94 with no acute changes noted.
Blood sugar reading is 109 mg/dL as tested upon initial contact.
Oxygen saturation is at 96% with no desaturation noted recently.
EKG is currently showing sinus rhythm with no acute abnormalities.
Did you find the patient's name or age with him, by any chance?
Were there any other symptoms or signs you noticed at the time of the incident?
Yes-he has a medical bracelet stating he has epilepsy.
Does he currently take any prescribed medications for this condition?
They mentioned clobazam or Onfi, if I remember correctly.
There is no active report of pain or discomfort provided at this time.
He has minor trauma to both knees, likely from the falls observed earlier.
During the seizure, his eyes were twitching and fixed toward the right with no clear visual response to commands.
His GCS was not obtainable at first, but now he is beginning to respond to loud stimuli.
There is no head deformation or bleeding described on scene assessment up to this time.
We are administering oxygen at 6 liters per minute via nasal cannula to support his oxygen saturation.
His pulse has decreased to 82 now, earlier was 98.
Respiratory rate is up to 24 now-still regular and without effort.
Blood pressure came down slightly but remains at 144 over 94.
Blood glucose is now reading 0. I'll note this as consistent with current status.
His oxygen saturation is still at 95%, a minor drop but still acceptable.
EKG remains in sinus rhythm with no new changes from earlier readings.
We're placing the patient on ECG 4-lead monitoring now for continuous observation.
IV access has been established for potential interventions.
Initiate protocol for medical-seizure as outlined in protocol 3-12 for field treatment.
He appears to be beginning to regain consciousness and is intermittently responding to verbal stimuli now.
Patient has been secured to the stretcher and is being moved into the transport unit.
IV, O2, and ECG are all in place and functioning for transport.
He is now more aware but remains in an altered mental state consistent with post-seizure.
Our report has been given to the nurse at the emergency department upon arrival.
The patient arrived as A&O x0 and had a self-limited seizure lasting 30 to 35 seconds; vitals and interventions documented.
Once he regains full consciousness, we can discuss more, but we are now en route to the hospital.
The stretcher is now in the ambulance unit and secured during transport.
[COMPANY_NAME] has been selected as the destination per protocol.
The patient is safely inside and secured for transport.
Thank you for your help. We'll follow up as needed post-hospital visit.
